33370: Glioblastomas are very serious brain tumors that have different types of cells, including special cells called glioblastoma stem cells. These stem cells can make more of themselves and help the tumor grow. They work differently from other cells in the tumor. We found that a protein called A20, which helps cells live longer and controls something called NF-kappaB, is more common in these stem cells compared to regular tumor cells. To see if A20 is important for the stem cells, we used a special tool to reduce its amount in the stem cells. When we did this, the stem cells grew and lived less well. This happened because they couldn't go through their normal cycle as much and had less protection against being damaged. The stem cells also became more sensitive to a substance called TNFalpha, which can cause them to die. Fewer stem cells meant the tumor had a harder time making new cells, especially when grown in lab dishes. Mice with human tumor cells that had less A20 lived longer. Looking at information from people with glioblastomas showed that those with more A20 tend to have better outcomes. Targeting A20 could be a way to fight these deadly brain tumors. 123859:
Researchers used a special imaging technique to watch kidney cells called podocytes and another type of cell called parietal epithelial cells (PECs) move around inside a living mouse. They developed new mice that had special glowing tags to help them see these cells better. When they looked at the cells, they saw that podocytes sometimes came together in small groups and moved into a part of the kidney called Bowman's capsule. They also noticed that multiple podocytes moved at the same time. In another group of mice, they observed PECs moving towards podocytes and forming tiny connections between them. This shows that the kidney is more active and changing than previously thought. This new way of watching kidney cells could help scientists learn more about how the kidneys get hurt and heal.

306006: The strength of T cell response depends on how well T cells and their targets connect. When T cells meet their target, they use a special part called the T cell receptor to recognize pieces from the target. It's not clear what makes some pieces better at activating T cells. We found that a piece that usually doesn't work very well can actually make T cells grow more than a piece that usually works well. Using computer models, we thought this might be because the poor piece couldn't form an important group needed for full activation. This idea was proven by experiments that showed making this group bigger reduced the poor piece's ability to activate T cells. Our research shows that many different things work together to decide how good a T cell target is. The strength of T cell response depends on how well T cells and their targets connect. 464511:
Memory and how cells decide their fate Even cells that are exactly the same and live in the same place can look and act very differently. It's not always clear why they do this—whether it's just luck, outside influences, or the cells making their own choices. To understand this better, we watched thousands of tiny living things called Bacillus subtilis for many, many generations without changing anything. We noticed that some of these cells moved around freely, while others stuck together in chains. The cells that moved didn't seem to remember how long they had been moving or try to control it. But the cells that stayed in chains did a good job of sticking together for a set amount of time. This shows that the cells have different ways of deciding what to do. We found out that there are three special parts inside the cells that help them make these decisions. These parts work separately, so one part can start the process of sticking together, while another part keeps it going. When something outside the cell starts this process, it can lead to the cells forming a protective group called a biofilm. Our findings suggest that the cells can try out being part of a group on their own, and outside influences can make them stick together longer if needed.

791050: The study looked at whether breathing in tiny air pollutants from the past might make some women more anxious today. We studied over 71,000 women who took part in a big health survey called the Nurses' Health Study. These women lived across the United States and we knew how much air pollution they were exposed to in the past and how they felt about being anxious. In 2004, we asked them questions to find out if they had high levels of anxiety. On average, these women were around 70 years old. We found that the more tiny air pollutants they breathed in, especially in the last month, the more likely they were to have high anxiety levels. This suggests that breathing in these tiny pollutants might be linked to feeling more anxious. The study focused on two types of tiny air pollutants: ones that are 2.5 micrometers or smaller (PM2.5) and ones that are between 2.5 and 10 micrometers (PM2.5-10). We also considered how close their homes were to busy roads, which can be a source of air pollution. Overall, the study showed that higher exposure to these tiny air pollutants in the past was connected to having more anxiety symptoms. The results suggest that reducing exposure to air pollution might help lower anxiety levels. The study did not prove that air pollution causes anxiety, but it hints at a possible link. More research is needed to understand this connection better. The study included mostly older women, so the findings may not apply to other groups. The researchers used a method to measure air pollution based on where people lived and how far they were from busy roads. They also used a specific test to measure anxiety levels. The study found that higher levels of tiny air pollutants were linked to higher chances of having anxiety. The exact amount of air pollution that caused more anxiety wasn't clear, but the study showed a pattern that suggested more pollution meant more anxiety. The study was observational, meaning it looked at what happened without trying to change anything. This means we can't say for sure that air pollution caused the anxiety, but it does suggest a possible link that needs further investigation. The study's main goal was to see if there was any connection between past exposure to tiny air pollutants and current anxiety levels in women. The results showed that higher exposure to these tiny pollutants was associated with more anxiety symptoms. The study highlights the importance of considering air pollution when looking at mental health issues like anxiety. It also suggests that efforts to reduce air pollution could potentially benefit mental health. 970012:
Cold exposure makes heart problems worse by making cholesterol particles smaller and causing more fat to build up in arteries. This happens even if you don't eat more when it's cold. In special mice that can't handle fats well, staying cold for a long time leads to bigger, unstable patches forming inside their arteries. These patches are more likely to break off and cause heart attacks or strokes. A protein called UCP1, which helps keep some mice warm, stops this from happening. When UCP1 is missing, the mice get more artery problems. Also, being cold makes less of a hormone called adiponectin, and giving this hormone to mice helps prevent these artery issues. This study shows why cold weather might be dangerous for people's hearts.

1049501:
Neutrophil extracellular traps, which are like webs made by white blood cells, often contain damaged pieces of DNA from inside the cells. These traps can cause the body to attack itself, similar to what happens in lupus. We don't fully understand how these traps are created or how they lead to inflammation without an infection. When certain parts of the cell called ribonucleoprotein immune complexes (RNP ICs) trigger the creation of these traps, the cell's powerhouses, known as mitochondria, become less active and move to the cell's outer layer. The damaged DNA from the mitochondria can be released outside the cell and causes inflammation. When this damaged DNA is put into mice, it triggers a response that leads to the production of a chemical called interferon, which is part of the body's defense system. People with a condition called systemic lupus erythematosus and those with another condition called chronic granulomatous disease, which affects the cell's ability to fight infections, also show signs of this damaged DNA being released and causing inflammation. Blocking the chemicals made by the mitochondria in mice helps reduce symptoms and interferon levels in a model of lupus. Overall, these results suggest that mitochondria play a key role in making both the traps and the damaged DNA that can lead to autoimmune diseases like lupus.

1084345:
Restoring a process that helps remove old or damaged proteins in older liver improves how well the liver works. This process, called chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), is like a trash disposal system inside cells that gets less effective as we get older. We found that this system stops working well in older animals, leading to more damaged proteins and problems in the body. In this study, we tried to keep this trash disposal system working well even as the animals got older. We created special mice that could control how much of the key part of this system was present. By keeping this key part active, we saw that the cells had fewer damaged proteins, were better at dealing with protein damage, and the liver worked better. This suggests that keeping this important cleanup process working could help prevent some of the problems that come with getting older.

1122279:
Exercise makes blood vessels in the heart work better in pigs. In some parts of the heart, the blood vessels don't relax as well when they should. We wanted to see if exercising could help. We did an experiment with female pigs where we blocked one part of their heart for two months. Then we let half of them run on a treadmill for 16 weeks, while the other half stayed in cages. After that, we looked at tiny blood vessels in the heart. The blood vessels that didn't get enough blood because of the blockage didn't relax as well in the group that didn't exercise. But in the group that exercised, the blood vessels relaxed just as well as those that weren't affected by the blockage. N

1148122:
Understanding how organisms change to better survive in certain conditions is a big question in science. It's hard to figure out exactly what parts of an organism change because many different parts might be involved, and each part could play a small role. We created a way to look at both experiments and computer models together to solve this problem. We used this method to learn how E. coli bacteria can tolerate more ethanol. We tested how changing one part of the bacteria at a time affects its ability to handle ethanol. Then, using computers, we looked at how all these changes fit together to form bigger processes inside the bacteria, like how they control water balance or build their outer shell. Surprisingly, we found that a key way the bacteria adapt is by changing how they process and use ethanol inside them. By studying the bacteria that can handle more ethanol after being grown in the lab, we learned about natural ways bacteria can become more tolerant to ethanol. These bacteria mostly followed similar paths to adapt as we predicted by looking at the overall picture of what helps them survive.

1180972: A study looked at how genes affect being overweight in grown-ups who were adopted away from their birth parents when they were very young. These grown-ups were then compared to their brothers and sisters who stayed with their birth parents. The adopted grown-ups were put into four groups based on their weight: thin, normal, overweight, and very overweight. We measured the weight and height of 115 full brothers and sisters of 57 adopted grown-ups, and 850 half brothers and sisters of 341 adopted grown-ups. The weight of the full brothers and sisters went up as the weight of the adopted grown-ups increased. For half brothers and sisters, their weight also went up, but not as much. There was no big difference in these results based on whether the adopted grown-ups or their brothers and sisters were boys or girls. The weight of full brothers and sisters of adopted grown-ups who were overweight or very overweight was much higher than those whose adopted grown-ups were normal weight. This suggests that genes play a role in how overweight someone is, even if they grow up in the same home, and this can happen through many small genes or one big gene. 1215116:
Over the last 20 years, there have been big improvements in fighting some serious tropical illnesses that affect people in poor, rural areas of developing countries. These diseases, like river blindness, elephantiasis, and others, can make it hard for people to work and can cause shame. For a long time, these illnesses have been a part of life for many communities. But now, thanks to special programs that focus on one disease at a time, there's hope that we can stop these illnesses from spreading in some places. In fact, we might even be able to completely get rid of one of them, guinea worm. These diseases are common in poor, rural areas where there's no money to develop treatments. However, when we do treat them, it usually works well.

1469751:
A special type of tiny ball called aptamer-functionalized lipid nanoparticles was made to target bone-making cells called osteoblasts. This was done to deliver a specific piece of genetic information, called siRNA, that can turn off a protein called Plekho1. This protein is important for bone growth. We used a special molecule called aptamer CH6 to make sure these tiny balls only went to osteoblasts. When these tiny balls reached the osteoblasts, they released the siRNA inside. This siRNA turned off the Plekho1 protein, which helped the bones grow stronger and healthier. We tested this on animals with weak bones and healthy ones, and it worked well. This shows that these tiny balls could be a new way to help bones grow stronger using genetic information, improving how we treat bone problems.

1471041:
In people with celiac disease, their body makes special antibodies against a protein called transglutaminase 2 (TG2) when they eat foods containing gluten. These people have a certain gene that makes them more likely to get celiac disease. We studied the antibodies in their small intestine to understand how they form. We found that there are many more antibodies against TG2 in the small intestine of people with active celiac disease compared to healthy people. These antibodies stick strongly to TG2 but don't change much after being made. Unlike antibodies made during infections, these antibodies haven't grown quickly recently and won't last long outside the body. This suggests that the body has a ready-made set of antibodies that can quickly react to TG2. When these antibodies are made as different types, some stick to TG2 better than others. This might explain why the body responds so strongly to TG2 in celiac disease.
In people with celiac disease, their bodies make special antibodies against a protein called transglutaminase 2 (TG2) when they eat foods containing gluten. People with celiac disease have a specific gene that makes them more prone to developing this condition. To understand how these antibodies form, we studied the antibodies in their small intestines. We discovered that there are significantly more antibodies against TG2 in the small intestines of people with active celiac disease compared to those without the condition. These antibodies strongly attach to TG2 but do not undergo significant changes after being produced. Unlike antibodies formed during infections, these antibodies have not recently grown rapidly and will not survive well outside the body. This indicates that the body has a pre-existing set of antibodies that can quickly react to TG2. When these antibodies are produced in different forms, some versions stick to TG2 better than others. This might help explain why the body reacts so strongly to TG2 in celiac disease.
1568684: The special kind of fat called brown fat in people can help burn extra calories. Scientists found out that a substance called chenodeoxycholic acid, which is part of the digestive juice bile, can make brown fat more active. They gave 12 women this substance for two days and saw their brown fat working harder. This made them burn more energy overall. When they tested it in the lab on actual pieces of brown fat from people, they found that it made the fat cells use energy faster and make more of a special protein. This didn't happen when they tested it on regular white fat cells. This shows that bile acids might be a way to make brown fat work better in people.
1606628:
In 1990 and 2015, we looked at how many young kids around the world were very thin. The United Nations wanted to cut the number of very thin kids in half by 2015. We studied how being very thin changed over time in different parts of the world. We used information from about 31 million children under 5 years old who took part in health surveys in 139 countries from 1965 to 2002. We found that worldwide, the number of very thin kids went down from 26.5% in 1990 to 17.6% in 2015. In richer countries, it dropped from 1.6% to 0.9%. In poorer areas, it fell from 30.2% to 19.3%. The overall goal was to see if progress was made in reducing very thin kids over those years.

1642727:
The study looked at how exercise might help older people who are having trouble with their memory but aren't diagnosed with Alzheimer's. We wanted to see if being more active could stop their thinking skills from getting worse. We did a big experiment where we asked 170 people to either keep doing what they normally did or start exercising at home for 24 weeks. After 18 months, we checked how well their thinking had changed. People who exercised showed less decline in their thinking skills compared to those who didn't change their routine. But the difference wasn't big enough to be sure it was because of the exercise.
To clarify the last part about the results: Even though the people who exercised showed less decline, the change wasn't large enough to say for certain that the exercise was the reason. It's like if you had two plants and one grew a little bit more, but you couldn't tell if it was because of extra water or sunlight. More research would be needed to be sure. Effect of exercise on memory in older people with memory issues but no Alzheimer's: a study. We wanted to know if moving around more could slow down how much their thinking gets worse. We did a study where 170 people, mostly over 50, who said they had trouble remembering things but weren't diagnosed with Alzheimer's, were split into two groups. One group kept doing what they usually did, and the other started exercising at home for 24 weeks. After 18 months, we checked how their thinking had changed. People who exercised showed less decline in their thinking skills, but the difference wasn't big enough to be sure it was because of the exercise. It's like if one plant grew a little more, but we can't be sure if it was because of extra water or sunlight. More research would be needed to be sure. 1805641: In areas where malaria is common, a type of medicine called artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) can help reduce how easily the disease spreads. This study looked at how well ACT and other medicines might work in six places with different levels of malaria. To do this, researchers created a computer model to show what would happen if these medicines were used more widely. They used information from a survey of over 5,000 people to estimate how many people had the malaria parasite and how often they took their medicine. The goal was to see how much these medicines could lower the number of people who could spread malaria to mosquitoes. The study focused on understanding how different types of malaria treatments affect the ability of infected people to pass the disease to mosquitoes. 1834762: Commensal and disease-causing bacteria living in our bodies can affect our weight, risk of cancer, and autoimmune conditions like lupus, even though we don't fully understand how. We discovered that when these bacteria form communities called biofilms, they create structures made from proteins and DNA. Specifically, we found that a protein called curli, which is part of these biofilm structures, combines with DNA to form strong fibers. This combination speeds up the process of protein clumping together and creates complex structures that can activate parts of our immune system. Certain immune cells, like dendritic cells, become more active and release chemicals called cytokines, some of which can be harmful in lupus. When we gave these fiber-DNA structures to mice, it caused their immune systems to react and produce antibodies against their own body parts, similar to what happens in lupus. We also saw that when lupus-prone mice were infected with bacteria that make curli, they produced more of these harmful antibodies than when infected with bacteria that don't make curli. This research suggests that the types of bacteria in our gut and their ability to form biofilms might play a role in causing or worsening autoimmune diseases like lupus, and could help us find new ways to treat these conditions. We discovered that when these bacteria form communities called biofilms, they create structures made from proteins and DNA. Specifically, we found that a protein called curli, which is part of these biofilm structures, combines with DNA to form strong fibers. We also saw that when lupus-prone mice were infected with bacteria that make curli, they produced more of these harmful antibodies than when infected with bacteria that don't make curli. This research suggests that the types of bacteria in our gut and their ability to form biofilms might play a role in causing or worsening autoimmune diseases like lupus, and could help us find new ways to treat these conditions.
1897324:
A study using a special test found some new parts of a signaling system that helps control how much an organ grows and keeps stem cells active. This system is called the Hippo-YAP pathway. We used a method that looks at many different types of cell signals to find these new parts. We discovered that a protein called LKB1, which usually stops tumors from growing, works by changing other proteins in the system. LKB1 affects where another protein called Scribble is found in the cell and how well a key part of the Hippo-YAP system works. We also learned that the protein YAP is important for LKB1 to do its job of stopping tumors. Our findings show how LKB1 and YAP are connected and could help in treating cancers caused by changes in LKB1. They also give us clues about what starts the Hippo-YAP system working.

1982286: Reverse engineering of TLX oncogenic transcriptional networks identifies RUNX1 as tumor suppressor in T-ALL The TLX1 and TLX3 genes play a big part in causing T cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL). We used a technique to understand how these genes work together to cause the disease. This helped us see that TLX1 and TLX3 are important leaders in a group of genes that control T-ALL. A special analysis showed that RUNX1 is a key player in the disease caused by TLX1 and TLX3, and suggested that RUNX1 helps stop tumors from growing. Our study also found that people with T-ALL often have changes in their RUNX1 gene. Overall, our findings show that TLX1 and TLX3 are very important in causing T-ALL, demonstrate how analyzing networks can help find key parts of the disease, and suggest that RUNX1 stops T-ALL from developing. TLX1 and TLX3 are at the top of a group of genes that control the growth of leukemia, and RUNX1 helps prevent T-ALL. TLX1 and TLX3 are like the bosses of a group of genes that cause T-ALL. 2014909:
Oncogenic signals from cancer cells attract special immune cells called myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) to help start tumors. These immune cells play a big part in both the main tumor and spreading to other parts of the body. Doctors don't fully understand why some people with the same kind of cancer have different levels of these immune cells. By looking at human cancer samples and using mice with similar types of tumors, researchers found that when cancer cells have strong signals from something called mTOR, it makes the tumor attract more of these special immune cells. They also found that blocking these signals or things that make them stronger can slow down tumor growth. But if you give back these immune cells or a substance they produce, the tumor growth can come back. The cancer cells that start tumors make more of this substance. These immune cells then make more of the starting cells in the tumor, creating a cycle. Looking at real breast cancer samples and growing them in mice showed that these ideas work in people too. This study shows that mTOR signals help cancer attract harmful immune cells and explains how certain groups of cancer cells might change to need a specific mix of immune cells to grow.

2095573:
LDL cholesterol levels: a big search for genes
BACKGROUND: High LDL cholesterol is linked to heart problems. Learning more about how our bodies handle and control LDL cholesterol could help find new ways to treat it.
We did a big search to find genes related to LDL cholesterol levels.
METHODS: We looked at blood samples from over 11,000 people to measure their LDL cholesterol. We checked more than 290,000 spots on their DNA that are common enough to study. We also used data from another set of tests on around 4,000 people to look at more than 290,000 spots. Then, we tested these findings in two other groups of people to make sure they were right.
We used special math tricks to find the most important spots and combined all the results to see how each spot affects LDL cholesterol levels.
FINDINGS: We found two spots in one part of the DNA that seem to be strongly linked to LDL cholesterol levels. Another test found a third spot that was also linked. These spots might give us clues about how to better understand and treat high LDL cholesterol.
2119889:
Characterizing two types of tiny chemical blockers that stop a specific group of proteins from making new thread-like structures called actin filaments. These proteins, known as Arp2/3 complex, help cells move in many ways. Scientists want to know how much these proteins contribute to how nerve cells grow and find paths, but they can't easily stop them from working inside living cells. We found two kinds of tiny chemicals that can do this. One stops the proteins from moving properly, and the other changes their shape. Both prevent certain bacteria and immune cells from making long, tail-like structures made of actin filaments. Having two different ways to stop these proteins gives scientists a strong tool to study them in living cells.

2177022: Immovable patches of a chemical signal and free-floating versions of the same signal work together to guide the movement of special immune cells called dendritic cells. These signals tell the cells to move in a certain way or to move randomly and stick to surfaces. By studying how dendritic cells move, we found that their movement depended on how these signals were presented in the body. When a fixed version of a specific signal called CCL21 was present, it made the dendritic cells move randomly but only on the surface where the signal was. This happened because the cells stuck to the surface. When the dendritic cells touched CCL21 directly, they cut off the part of the signal that would normally make them stick to surfaces, allowing the signal to float freely. Another similar signal, also without sticking parts, made the cells move towards it but didn't make them stick. The combination of sticking and moving helped the cells move in a dynamic but limited way, much like what happens in places where the immune system is active.
2251426:
MicroRNAs: A Safety Net Against Stress? Recent findings show that microRNAs (miRNAs) play a key role in helping cells deal with stress, beyond their known job in development. Interestingly, miRNAs, which usually turn down the production of certain genes, can sometimes turn them up during stressful situations. This might happen because miRNAs team up with other molecules in new ways when cells face stress, causing these molecules to move around inside the cell differently than usual.

2356950: MBD1, a protein that controls how genes are turned on or off, helps manage how neural stem cells grow and change into other types of brain cells. In our previous study, we found that not having enough MBD1 can stop these stem cells from turning into new brain cells properly, but we didn't know why. Now, we've discovered that MBD1 controls the production of a small piece of genetic material called miR-184. When there's too much miR-184, it makes the stem cells grow more but prevents them from changing into different brain cells. By stopping miR-184, we could fix the problems caused by lacking MBD1. We also found that miR-184 affects another piece of genetic material called Numbl, which plays a role in brain development. MiR-184 does this by attaching to Numbl's instructions and changing how they're used. Adding extra Numbl can correct the issues caused by too much miR-184 or not enough MBD1. So, MBD1, miR-184, and Numbl work together to help neural stem cells decide whether to grow or turn into different brain cells. 2425364:
The relationship between how much vitamin D mom has in her blood during pregnancy and how the baby grows and is born: A big review of many studies. We looked at 3357 studies and found 31 that were useful. Low amounts of vitamin D in mom's blood seemed linked to a higher chance of the mom having gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, and having a baby who is smaller than expected for their age at birth. The baby's weight, length, and head size might also be affected.

2565138: Hyaluronic acid makes it hard for drugs to reach tumors and affects how blood flows in a type of cancer called pancreatic cancer. In this study, researchers used a special mouse model to look at how removing hyaluronic acid could help. They gave the mice a treatment that breaks down hyaluronic acid and then looked at how well blood could flow to the tumor and how well drugs could get inside. They also tested if combining this treatment with another common cancer drug could work better. The results showed that removing hyaluronic acid helped blood vessels grow back in the tumor and allowed more drugs to enter. Combining this treatment with the other drug slowed down the tumor's growth and made the mice live longer. This suggests that removing hyaluronic acid might be a useful way to treat pancreatic cancer. The researchers think that breaking down hyaluronic acid could be a new approach to improve drug delivery and treatment in pancreatic cancer. 2831620:
Protein that gets added or removed with acetic acid can change how it works, and this is part of how our bodies control things. This process, called acetylation, happens when acetic acid is moved from one place to another on certain proteins. Special enzymes called acetyltransferases and deacetylases control this process. Changes in acetylation can affect how our body uses food, makes energy, and grows new blood vessels. When these changes go wrong, it can lead to problems like being overweight, having diabetes, or heart issues. We call these kinds of problems "metabolic complications." As we learn more about which proteins get acetylated, we're finding out that this process also affects how our immune system works and can play a role in diseases like Alzheimer's and Huntington's. This review looks at what we know about how acetylation helps control metabolism and leads to diseases related to how our body uses food and energy.

2988714:
Local making and breaking down of proteins control how the tip of a growing nerve changes shape in response to signals. Only certain proteins are made right at the tip, while others travel from the cell body. It's not clear why just some proteins are made locally. Now, we've found that when the tip of a growing nerve gets a signal, it starts breaking down specific proteins, like RhoA, which helps the nerve change shape. If we stop the breakdown of RhoA, the nerve doesn't need to make new proteins to change shape. We also found that the main proteins broken down at the tip are those made there. So, breaking down proteins at the tip is important and makes sure the nerve can respond properly to signals by needing to make new proteins locally.

3203590:
In our study, we looked at how different hormones interact with specific proteins inside cells. The protein RXR can team up with other proteins like T3R and RAR to form pairs. We wanted to know if these pairs could respond to more than one type of hormone. By testing each protein alone and when paired with RXR, we found that RXR's ability to stick to DNA is hidden when it teams up with T3R or RAR. However, RXR can still work well with another type of protein called NGFI-B/Nurr1 without needing to stick to DNA. When RXR pairs with an always-active version of NGFI-B/Nurr1, they can respond to hormones in a new way. Our results show that when proteins team up, they can create new and unique ways to react to hormones. This suggests that how proteins interact is very important for how cells respond to hormones.

3315558:
In a study looking at why some kids in Italy are overweight, we checked the genes of 67 families where kids were found to be obese and compared them to 112 families where kids were not overweight. We did several tests to see if there are certain genes that might make someone more likely to be overweight. Our results show that genes do play a role, and suggest there might be one gene that is particularly important, even though its effect is not very strong.

3441524: Human TRPML1 channel shapes when it's open or closed TRPML1 is a special kind of channel in our cells that helps control how much calcium goes in and out of tiny parts called lysosomes. When this channel doesn't work right, people can get a serious illness called mucolipidosis type IV. In this study, scientists used a powerful microscope to take detailed pictures of the human TRPML1 channel in two different states - when it's closed and when it's open. They found that some parts of the channel change shape when it opens up, creating more space for certain molecules to fit inside. This is different from how another similar channel works. Understanding these differences could help us learn more about how the channel activates and might even help find ways to treat the illness caused by TRPML1 not working properly. Human TRPML1 channel takes different shapes when it's open or closed. TRPML1 is a special channel in our cells that controls how much calcium moves in and out of tiny parts called lysosomes. Problems with this channel can cause a serious illness called mucolipidosis type IV. Scientists used a powerful microscope to take detailed pictures of the human TRPML1 channel in two states: closed and open. They discovered that some parts of the channel change shape when it opens, making room for specific molecules. This is unlike how another similar channel works. Knowing these differences can help us understand how the channel activates and may lead to treatments for the illness caused by TRPML1 not functioning correctly.
3475317:
In human tuberculosis, there are areas called granulomas which are a sign of the disease. Doctors don't fully understand how they form or what they do. To learn more about granulomas, we looked at the proteins inside them without any preconceived ideas. We used special tools to carefully cut out parts of the granulomas, then studied them using powerful machines to see what proteins were present. We also used a microscope to take detailed pictures. We noticed that the center of the granulomas had a lot of things that make inflammation happen, like substances that fight infections and chemicals that can cause swelling. But the area around a part called caseum was less likely to cause inflammation. This pattern was seen in six people and some rabbits too. Even though the whole body has a mix of things that can either fight or cause inflammation, in each granuloma, these things are kept separate from each other. Based on our findings, we think that the way tuberculosis makes you sick might depend on where different parts of the inflammatory response are located as the granuloma forms.

3553087: Cigarette smoke can cause lung problems like bronchitis and emphysema in mice. These lung issues are often linked to both smoking and genes. In our study, we found that a protein called IRP2 might play a big role in making people more likely to get these lung problems. Mice without IRP2 were less affected by cigarette smoke.
3863543:
Mesenchymal cells in the body can affect how blood-forming cells work and might lead to cancer. We studied mice with a condition called Shwachman-Diamond syndrome, which is similar to some human diseases that increase the risk of cancer. We found that changing these mesenchymal cells made the blood-forming cells have trouble using energy, caused them to produce too much harmful stuff, and made them think their DNA was damaged. By looking at both mouse and human cells, we discovered that an immune response involving p53, S100A8/9, and TLR signals often causes this kind of damage. When these signals were turned on in the mesenchymal cells, it could predict whether someone would develop cancer or not, especially in a condition called myelodysplastic syndrome. Overall, our results suggest that stopping this immune response in the mesenchymal cells could help prevent cancer in people with these types of blood disorders.

3898784:
In a big study of people who had bleeding in their brain, researchers looked at how many people died while in the hospital based on whether they were taking certain blood thinners before the bleeding happened. They found that out of 141,311 people, 10.6% were taking one type of blood thinner called warfarin, 3.5% were taking another type called NOACs, and some were taking other medicines to thin their blood. The people taking these blood thinners were usually older and had more health problems like heart issues and past strokes. When the doctors looked at how severe the bleeding was, it didn't seem to be much different between the groups. The main thing they wanted to know was if the type of blood thinner someone was taking made a difference in how likely they were to die while in the hospital.

4347374: The human body has special defenses to fight off viruses like HIV. One of these defenses is a protein called APOBEC3G. To protect itself, HIV uses a protein called Vif to stop APOBEC3G from working properly. But when APOBEC3G is present, it can make HIV less able to spread by changing parts of the virus's genetic material. This change makes the virus more likely to make mistakes, which stops it from working right. APOBEC3G isn't just good at fighting HIV—it can also help defend against other types of viruses that use similar methods to copy their genetic material. This shows that APOBEC3G is a general defense system that helps protect the body from many different kinds of dangerous viruses.
